metadata.title: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
metadata.journal: American Journal of Physiology - Endocrinology and Metabolism
metadata.study_design: Experimental study with stable isotope labeled tracers measuring whole body glycerol and glucose kinetics at 14 and 22 hours of fasting
metadata.demographics: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat (24±2% and 23±2% body weight as fat, respectively)

evidence.quote: Gender differences in lipid and glucose kinetics during short-term fasting
BETTINA MITTENDORFER, JEFFREY F. HOROWITZ, AND SAMUEL KLEIN
Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 281: E1333–E1339, 2001
Six women and six men who were matched for adiposity (24±2 and 23±2% body wt as fat, respectively)
Substrate kinetics were measured by infusing stable isotope labeled tracers of glucose ([2H2]glucose) and glycerol ([2H5]glycerol)
We evaluated whole body lipid and glucose kinetics during basal (14-h fast) and short-term fasting (22-h fast) conditions

methods_participants.label: Participant N and demographics
methods_participants.value: Six men (32 ± 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 ± 2 yr old), matched on percent body fat

evidence.quote: Six men (32 6 4 yr old) and six premenopausal women (27 6 2 yr old), who were matched on percent body fat, participatedinthisstudy

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - health status
methods_participants.value: All subjects considered to be in good health after comprehensive medical evaluation including history, physical examination, electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

evidence.quote: All subjects were considered to be in good health after completing a comprehensive medical evaluation that included a history and physical examination, an electrocardiogram, and standard blood and urine tests

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - body weight and exercise
methods_participants.value: All subjects had stable body weight for ≥2 months and had been sedentary (regular exercise <1 h/wk) for ≥6 months before the study

evidence.quote: All subjects had a stable body weight for $2 mo and had been sedentary (regular exercise ,1h/wk)for$6mobeforethestudy
Each subject's fat mass (FM) and fat-free mass (FFM) were determined by dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (Hologic QDR1000/w, Waltham, MA)

methods_participants.label: Glycerol isotope infusion - infusion rate
methods_participants.value: [1,1,2,3,3-2H5]glycerol with priming dose of 1.8 mmol/kg and infusion rate of 0.12 mmol·kg body wt-1·min-1

evidence.quote: a constant infusion of [6,6-2H 2 ]glucose (priming dose: 18 mmol/kg; infusion rate: 0.22 mmolzkg body wt21zmin21; Cambridge Isotope Laboratories, Andover, MA) was started and maintained for 210 min

methods_participants.label: Pre-study liquid formula timing and composition
methods_participants.value: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing 12 kcal/kg body weight (55% carbohydrate, 30% fat, 15% protein)

evidence.quote: At 1800, subjects consumed a standard meal containing12kcal/kgbodywt(55%oftotalenergyascarbohydrate, 30% as fat, and 15% as protein)

methods_participants.label: Blood sampling - glucose and glycerol analysis

evidence.quote: Blood samples were collected in 1) chilled tubes containing EDTA to determine plasma fatty acid, glycerol, and glucose concentrations and glycerol and glucose enrichments

methods_participants.label: Fatty acid concentration measurement
methods_participants.value: Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) after [2-13C]glycerol was added as internal standard

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose concentration was determined by using an automated glucose analyzer (Yellow Springs Instrument, Yellow Springs, OH)

methods_participants.label: Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR measurement
methods_participants.value: GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column) with HFB anhydride derivatization and EI ionization

evidence.quote: Plasma glycerol and glucose TTR were determined by GC-MS (MSD 5973 system with capillary column; Hewlett-Packard) as previously described (22,23,40). Briefly, plasma proteins were precipitated with ice-cold acetone, and hexane was used to extract plasma lipids. The aqueous phase, containing glycerol and glucose, was dried by speed-vac centrifugation (Savant Instruments, Farmingdale, NY). Heptafluorobutyric (HFB) anhydride was used to form an HFB derivative of glycerol and glucose, and ions were produced by EI ionization

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with gender and time as factors, followed by post hoc comparisons with Student's t-test for multiple comparisons

evidence.quote: A two-way analysis of variance (ANOVA), with gender and time as factors, was used to test for significant differences in substrate kinetics between men and women during fasting. Significant F-ratios were followed by post hoc comparisons with the use of Student's t-test for multiple comparisons
A value of P #0.05 was considered to be statistically significant

narrative_results.statement: Basal glycerol rate of appearance in plasma was greater in women than in men

evidence.quote: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (Ra) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1; P < 0.05)

narrative_results.statement: Sexual dimorphism exists in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

evidence.quote: These data demonstrate the presence of sexual dimorphism in lipid, but not glucose, metabolism during both basal and short-term fasting conditions

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass_1 specifies 14 hours fasting duration; pass_2 describes background TTR blood sampling before isotope infusion - these are different measurements/timepoints, not the same fact

Pass 1 describes blood collection tubes and analytes for glucose/glycerol analysis. Pass 2 describes a different sampling interval (14-hour fast, 0930-1000). These are incompatible facts about different aspects.

Pass 1 describes blood collection tubes (EDTA and Trasylol) for hormone analysis. Pass 2 describes insulin measurement method (radioimmunoassay). These describe different aspects and are not directly comparable.
Pass 1 describes catecholamine collection tubes (reduced glutathione and EGTA). Pass 2 describes glucagon measurement method (radioimmunoassay). These are incompatible facts about different analytes.
Pass 1 states insulin measurement uses radioimmunoassay. Pass 2 states catecholamine measurement uses radioenzymatic method. These describe different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes glucagon measurement via radioimmunoassay; Pass 2 describes plasma fatty acid measurement via gas chromatography. These are completely different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes catecholamine measurement via radioenzymatic method; Pass 2 describes plasma glucose measurement via automated analyzer. Different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes glucose concentration measurement via automated analyzer; Pass 2 describes plasma glycerol measurement via GC-MS. Different analytes and methods.

Pass_1 states epinephrine was twice as high in women at 14h. Pass_2 states epinephrine increased in men but not women from 14-22h. These describe different aspects and potentially contradict regarding gender patterns.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (Ra) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1; P < 0.05)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Basal glycerol rate of appearance (R_a) in plasma, an indicator of whole body lipolytic rate, was greater in women than in men (2.1±0.2 vs. 1.5±0.1 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹; P < 0.05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: However, the relative increase in glycerol Ra with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: However, the relative increase in glycerol R_a with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men (40±7 vs. 80±4% increase; P < 0.05)

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The relative increase in glycerol Ra with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: The relative increase in glycerol rate of appearance with continued fasting was blunted in women compared with men

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In contrast, glucose Ra was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt−1·min−1)
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In contrast, glucose R_a was similar in men and women at 14 h (11±0.6 vs. 12±0.7 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹) and 22 h of fasting (9±0.6 vs. 10±0.6 mmol·kg body wt⁻¹·min⁻¹)

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Glucose rate of appearance was similar in men and women at both 14 and 22 hours of fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma insulin concentration was significantly lower in women than in men at 22 hours of fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma epinephrine concentration increased significantly in men but not in women from 14 to 22 h of fasting, resulting in similar plasma epinephrine concentrations with continued fasting in men and women

semantic_groups.rationale: Both describe identical inclusion criteria for health status with only minor wording difference ('considered in good health' vs 'considered to be in good health')
Both extractions are identical regarding medications and smoking inclusion criteria
Both extractions are identical regarding body weight stability and exercise sedentary requirements
Both extractions are identical regarding menstrual cycle phase inclusion criteria for female subjects
Same fact with minor wording difference: 'assessment method' vs 'measurement method' - functionally equivalent
Both describe glucose tracer infusion with same isotope and infusion rate (0.22 mmol/kg body wt/min); pass_2 omits priming dose but conveys same essential infusion information
Both describe glycerol tracer infusion with same isotope and infusion rate (0.12 mmol/kg body wt/min); pass_2 omits priming dose but conveys same essential infusion information
Pass_1 specifies 14 hours fasting duration; pass_2 describes background TTR blood sampling before isotope infusion - these are different measurements/timepoints, not the same fact
Both describe 22-hour fasting with blood sampling every 10 minutes between 1730-1800. Pass 2 provides more complete context about timing and measurements.
Both describe the same standard meal with identical composition (12 kcal/kg, 55% carb, 30% fat, 15% protein). Pass 2 adds timing detail (1800) but facts are equivalent.
Both describe Ensure formula with identical nutritional content (250 kcal, 40g carbs, 6.1g fat, 8.8g protein). Pass 2 adds timing (2000) but facts are equivalent.

Pass 1 describes blood collection tubes and analytes for glucose/glycerol analysis. Pass 2 describes a different sampling interval (14-hour fast, 0930-1000). These are incompatible facts about different aspects.

Pass 1 describes blood collection tubes (EDTA and Trasylol) for hormone analysis. Pass 2 describes insulin measurement method (radioimmunoassay). These describe different aspects and are not directly comparable.
Pass 1 describes catecholamine collection tubes (reduced glutathione and EGTA). Pass 2 describes glucagon measurement method (radioimmunoassay). These are incompatible facts about different analytes.
Pass 1 contains information about plasma processing (centrifugation within 30 minutes, storage at -70°C). Pass 2 is empty.
Pass 1 states insulin measurement uses radioimmunoassay. Pass 2 states catecholamine measurement uses radioenzymatic method. These describe different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes glucagon measurement via radioimmunoassay; Pass 2 describes plasma fatty acid measurement via gas chromatography. These are completely different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes catecholamine measurement via radioenzymatic method; Pass 2 describes plasma glucose measurement via automated analyzer. Different analytes and methods.
Pass 1 describes glucose concentration measurement via automated analyzer; Pass 2 describes plasma glycerol measurement via GC-MS. Different analytes and methods.

Both describe glycerol measurement via GC-MS with isotope internal standard. Pass 2 adds more instrument detail (MSD 5973 system) and mentions combined glucose TTR measurement, but core glycerol method is the same.
Pass 1 describes fatty acid concentration measurement; Pass 2 is empty/null. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Pass 1 describes tracer-to-tracee ratio determination via GC-MS; Pass 2 is empty/null. Orphan entry from Pass 1.
Both describe two-way ANOVA with gender and time factors, followed by post hoc Student's t-test. Minor wording variation in abbreviation (Two-way ANOVA vs ANOVA) but same method.
Both specify identical statistical significance threshold of P ≤ 0.05.
Both describe identical participant demographics (6 men, 6 premenopausal women with same ages). Pass_2 adds matching criterion (percent body fat) not mentioned in pass_1, but core facts are the same.

Both describe the same finding: glycerol Ra increase is blunted in women vs men during fasting. 'Ra' vs 'rate of appearance' is standard abbreviation expansion.

Both convey identical finding: glucose Ra similar between genders at 14h and 22h fasting. Minor wording differences ('at 14h and 22h' vs 'at both 14 and 22 hours') and 'Ra' abbreviation are negligible.
Identical factual content about sexual dimorphism in lipid but not glucose metabolism. Pass_1 includes 'gender difference' tag which is metadata, not a factual difference.
Same finding: plasma insulin lower in women at 22h fasting. Only difference is '22h' vs '22 hours' which is minor wording variation.
